| `tracemalloc.get_traced_memory()` | Returns `(current, peak)` memory in bytes |

---


<div style="page-break-after: always;"></div>

## 16.6 Summary — Python Memory Management Flow

```
Object created
      │
      ▼
Reference count = 1
      │
   +──┴──────────────────+
   │  New reference added │  → count++
   │  Reference deleted   │  → count--
   +─────────────────────+
      │
   count == 0?
   ┌──┴──┐
  YES    NO
   │      └──→ Object stays alive
   ▼
Memory freed immediately
(unless part of a cycle)
      │
   Cycle?
   ┌──┴──┐
  YES    NO
   │      └──→ Already freed above
   ▼
Cyclic GC detects
unreachable group
      │
      ▼
gc.collect() frees them
```


<div style="page-break-after: always;"></div>

---

# 17. Flask Framework

> **"Flask is a web framework which is created with the Python programming language."**

Flask is a **lightweight, micro web framework** for Python. It is called "micro" because it keeps the core simple but extensible — it does not include built-in tools like a database layer or form validation, giving you the freedom to choose your own tools and libraries.

Flask is built on two core components:
1. **WSGI** — Web Server Gateway Interface
2. **Jinja2** — Template Engine

---

![Flask Framework Overview](data:image/png;base64,)

---

## 17.1 WSGI — Web Server Gateway Interface

**WSGI** (pronounced "wiz-gee") is a **protocol** — a specification that defines how a web server communicates with a Python web application.

Without WSGI, every web server (Apache, Nginx, IIS) would need to know the specific internal details of every Python framework (Flask, Django, FastAPI). WSGI solves this by defining a **single standard interface** between the two sides, so any WSGI-compatible server can work with any WSGI-compatible Python app.


<div style="page-break-after: always;"></div>

### How It Works — Request Flow

```
Client Requests
      │
      ├── Req 1  (/home)
      ├── Req 2
      └── Req 3
            │
            ▼
    ┌─────────────┐
    │  Web Server  │  ◄── Receives raw HTTP requests
    │  (handles    │       and serves static files
    │   traffic)  │
    └──────┬──────┘
           │  WSGI Protocol (the bridge)
           │  ◄──────────────────────────────►
           ▼
    ┌─────────────┐
    │   Web App   │  ◄── Flask handles the routes:
    │  (Flask)    │       /home
    └─────────────┘       /api
                          /functionalinas
```

### Web Server Options (WSGI-Compatible)

| Web Server | Common Use |
|---|---|
| **AWS EC2** | Cloud-hosted server on Amazon Web Services |
| **Azure App** | Cloud-hosted server on Microsoft Azure |
| **Apache** | Popular open-source web server |
| **IIS** | Internet Information Services — Microsoft's web server |

> **In development**, Flask runs its own built-in development server so you don't need Apache or Nginx. In **production**, you deploy behind a real WSGI server like Gunicorn or uWSGI, which sits in front of your Flask app.

---

![Flask WSGI Architecture Diagram](data:image/png;base64,)

---

## 17.2 Jinja2 — Template Engine

**Jinja2** is a **web template engine** for Python. It allows you to write HTML pages that are dynamically populated with real data at runtime — so instead of writing a separate HTML file for every user or every result, you write one **template** and Jinja2 fills it in.

### How It Works

```
Data Source  ──────────────►  Web Templates  ──►  Web Page (rendered HTML)
(backend)                      (HTML + Jinja2
                                syntax)
```

### Data Sources Jinja2 Can Pull From

| Source | Example |
|---|---|
| **SQL Database** | PostgreSQL, MySQL, SQLite |
| **CSV Sheet** | Pandas DataFrames, flat files |
| **ML Model** | Predictions, classification results |
| **MongoDB** | NoSQL document store |

### Real-World Page Examples

**ML Prediction Page** — A template with an image upload area and a text result box. The layout stays the same; the prediction result (e.g. "Cat") is injected dynamically by the ML model each time a user submits a photo.

**Login Page** — A template with username and password fields. The same HTML layout is reused for every user; whether the login succeeds is determined by checking the database dynamically.

---

![Jinja2 Template Engine Diagram](data:image/png;base64,)

---

## 17.3 Jinja2 Template Syntax

Jinja2 extends plain HTML with special tags:

| Syntax | Purpose | Example |
|---|---|---|
| `{{ variable }}` | Output a variable value | `{{ username }}` |
| `{% if ... %}` | Conditional logic | `{% if user.is_admin %}` |
| `{% for ... %}` | Loop over a list | `{% for item in items %}` |
| `{% extends "base.html" %}` | Inherit a base template layout | Avoids repeating header/footer |
| `{% block content %}` | Define a replaceable section | Used with `extends` |
| `{{- -}}` | Strip whitespace | Cleaner output |

### Example — Dynamic HTML Template

```html
<!-- templates/index.html -->
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html>
<body>
    <h1>Hello, {{ name }}!</h1>

    {% if items %}
        <ul>
        {% for item in items %}
            <li>{{ item }}</li>
        {% endfor %}
        </ul>
    {% else %}
        <p>No items found.</p>
    {% endif %}
</body>
</html>
```

```python
# app.py — Flask passes data into the template
from flask import Flask, render_template

app = Flask(__name__)

@app.route('/')
def home():
    return render_template('index.html', name='Sher', items=['Python', 'Flask', 'ML'])
```

---

## 17.4 Flask — Minimal Working App

```python
from flask import Flask

app = Flask(__name__)   # Creates the Flask application instance

@app.route('/')         # Decorator maps the URL '/' to this function
def home():
    return 'Hello, World!'

@app.route('/api')
def api():
    return '{"status": "ok"}'

if __name__ == '__main__':
    app.run(debug=True)  # Starts the built-in development server
                         # debug=True enables auto-reload on code changes
```

> **How routing works:** The `@app.route('/path')` decorator tells Flask — "when a request comes in for this URL, run this function and return its response." This maps directly to the `/home`, `/api`, `/functionalinas` routes shown in the architecture diagram.

---


<div style="page-break-after: always;"></div>

## 17.5 Flask Architecture — Full Picture

```
                    BROWSER / CLIENT
                          │
              ┌───────────┼───────────┐
           Req 1        Req 2       Req 3
              └───────────┼───────────┘
                          ▼
               ┌─────────────────────┐
               │     Web Server      │
               │  (Gunicorn/Apache/  │
               │   AWS EC2/Azure)    │
               └──────────┬──────────┘
                          │  WSGI Protocol
                          ▼
               ┌─────────────────────┐
               │    Flask App        │
               │                     │
               │  @route('/home')    │
               │  @route('/api')     │
               │  @route('/...')     │
               └──────────┬──────────┘
                          │
               ┌──────────┴──────────┐
               │                     │
        Jinja2 Templates         Business Logic
        (HTML rendering)         (Python functions,
               │                  DB queries, ML)
               ▼                     │
          Web Page              Data Sources
         (sent back             (SQL, CSV, ML
          to client)             Model, MongoDB)
```

---


<div style="page-break-after: always;"></div>

## 17.6 Quick Reference — Flask Core Concepts

| Concept | What It Is | Why It Matters |
|---|---|---|
| **Flask** | Python micro web framework | Lightweight, flexible, easy to get started |
| **WSGI** | Protocol between web server and Python app | Makes Flask deployable on any standard server |
| **Jinja2** | HTML template engine | Renders dynamic pages without duplicating HTML |
| **Route** | URL mapped to a Python function | Defines what happens when a URL is visited |
| **`render_template()`** | Loads and fills a Jinja2 template | Connects Python data to the HTML front-end |
| **`debug=True`** | Dev server auto-reloads on changes | Speeds up development — never use in production |

---


<div style="page-break-after: always;"></div>


---

## 17.7 Understanding Simple Flask App Skeleton

Flask is a lightweight **WSGI web framework** in Python. Every Flask application starts with a basic skeleton.

### Key Concepts

- **WSGI** *(Web Server Gateway Interface)* — A standard interface between web servers and Python web applications.
- **`Flask(__name__)`** — Creates an instance of the Flask class. The `__name__` parameter tells Flask where to look for the entry point of the application.
- **`if __name__ == "__main__"`** — This block ensures the app runs only when the script is executed directly (not when imported).

### Code

```python
from flask import Flask

# WSGI Application
app = Flask(__name__)
# Creates an instance of the Flask class.
# "__name__" is passed so Flask knows the entry point of the application.

if __name__ == "__main__":  # Execution starts from here
    app.run()
```

> **Note:** This is the **basic skeleton** of any Flask web application. Everything else builds on top of this structure.

---

## 17.8 Adding Routes to the Basic Skeleton

**Routes** define the URLs of your web application and map them to Python functions.

### Key Concepts

- **`@app.route("/")`** — A **decorator** that binds a URL path to a function. The `"/"` path represents the **home page**.
- **`debug=True`** — Enables **auto-restart** of the development server whenever code is updated. Without it, the server must be restarted manually after every change.
- **`app.run()`** — Starts the **local development server**. Should **not** be used in production.


<div style="page-break-after: always;"></div>

### Code

```python
from flask import Flask

app = Flask(__name__)

# Home page route — equivalent to "google.com/"
@app.route("/")
def welcome():
    return "Welcome to this Flask App"

# Index page route
@app.route("/index")
def index():
    return "Welcome to the index page"

if __name__ == "__main__":
    app.run(debug=True)
    # debug=True → server auto-restarts on code changes
```

### `app.run()` Parameters

| Parameter | Type | Description |
|---|---|---|
| `host` | `str \| None` | Hostname to listen on. Use `"0.0.0.0"` to make it externally visible. |
| `port` | `int \| None` | Port number for the server. |
| `debug` | `bool \| None` | Enables auto-reload and debugger. |
| `load_dotenv` | `bool` | Loads environment variables from `.env` file. Default: `True`. |

### Running the App

```bash
python ./routes.py
```

### Result

```
* Running on http://127.0.0.1:5000
* Debug mode: on
* Restarting with stat
```

> ⚠️ **Warning:** `app.run()` is for **development only**. It is not intended to meet the security and performance requirements of a production server. For production deployment, use a proper **WSGI server** (e.g., Gunicorn, uWSGI).

---

### Clean Version — Basic Skeleton + Routes (No Comments)

```python
from flask import Flask

app = Flask(__name__)

@app.route("/")
def welcome():
    return "Welcome to this Flask App"

@app.route("/index")
def index():
    return "Welcome to the index page"

if __name__ == "__main__":
    app.run(debug=True)
```

---
